content:
4 min read
Just now

--

Real-time sync, CRDTs, and the unglamorous security lesson nobody warns you about.
It started, like most of my side projects, with a small itch.
I wanted an AI dashboard that didn’t fall apart the moment my Wi-Fi blinked. One where two people could poke at the same thing at once and not overwrite each other. One that didn’t leak my API key to anyone who opened DevTools.
That’s it. That was the whole brief I gave myself. A few weekends later it had a name — AgentHub — and a personality of its own.
The three things I refused to compromise on
1. It has to work offline. Most “collaborative” apps are really just “online apps that nag you when you’re offline.” I wanted the opposite: a thing that treats the network as a nice-to-have. So every bit of state is persisted locally in IndexedDB. You can close the tab, lose connection, walk into a tunnel — when you come back, your state is already there. The network catching up is a background detail, not a blocking screen.
2. Collaboration without the merge headaches. This is where it got fun. Instead of fighting last-write-wins bugs, I leaned on CRDTs via Yjs. Conflict-free replicated data types are one of those ideas that feel like magic the first time they click: two people edit the same state, offline, on different machines — and the data just merges, deterministically, no conflicts, no “are you sure?” dialogs. Yjs + y-websocket for the live channel, y-indexeddb for the local durability. The two halves snap together beautifully.
3. The AI key never touches the browser. The app talks to a language model (I’m using Groq for the speed), but the API key stays server-side. The frontend calls my own backend; the backend proxies to the model. Single domain serves the React build, the API, and the WebSocket upgrade — one deploy, no CORS gymnastics in production.
On paper, I was done. It worked. It was kind of beautiful.
And then I tried to actually open-source it.
The part nobody puts in the tutorial
Here’s the humbling bit.
I was about to push the repo public when I made myself do something I usually skip: I read my own code like a stranger would. Specifically, the proxy.
And I realized my “secure” backend was a wide-open ATM for my API key.
Think about it. I’d kept the key off the client — pat on the back — but the proxy endpoint had no auth, no rate limit, and CORS open to the entire internet. Anyone who found the URL could pipe unlimited requests through it, on my dime, from any website on Earth. I’d locked the front door and left the vault open.
That stung in the good way. So before anything went public, I fixed it:

• An origin allowlist for CORS — with a loud startup warning if I forget to set it, because I will forget to set it.

• Per-IP rate limiting on the proxy, so one bad actor can’t drain a key in an afternoon.

• An optional bearer-token gate for when the app isn’t purely public.

• Logging gated behind a debug flag — because the original version happily logged every user’s prompt to the console. Fine on my laptop. Not fine in production.
None of this is glamorous. None of it shows up in a demo GIF. But it’s the difference between “a cool repo” and “a thing a stranger can safely run.”
What “open-source ready” actually means
I used to think open-sourcing was git push plus a README. It's not. The repo only felt honest once it had:

• A real LICENSE (turns out “it’s on GitHub” is not a license).

• A SECURITY.md that’s upfront about the proxy’s risks instead of hiding them.

• A CONTRIBUTING.md so the next person isn’t reverse-engineering my setup.

• A Quick Start you can copy-paste and have running in under five minutes.

• And the boring guarantee that no secret ever lived in the git history. Not the current commit — the entire history.
That last one is worth repeating to anyone reading: a key that was ever committed is a key that’s compromised, even if you delete it later. Check before you publish, not after.
What I’m taking with me
The features were the fun part. The CRDTs felt like wizardry. The offline-first sync still makes me grin when I yank the network cable and watch it shrug.
But the lesson that’ll outlast this project is the unglamorous one: shipping something for yourself and shipping something for strangers are two different jobs. The second one is mostly about respect — for the next developer’s time, and for everyone who’s going to trust your code enough to run it.
AgentHub is live and MIT-licensed now. If offline-first sync or CRDTs are on your “I should really learn that” list, the repo is a small, real, readable place to start.
👉 github.com/payallenka/AgentHub
If you build something with it — or spot something I missed — open an issue. That’s the whole point of putting it out there.

Thanks for reading. I write about the stuff I build and the things they teach me — follow along if that’s your kind of thing.
